skill.name: scientific-visualization
skill.description: Create publication-ready scientific figures with journal-aware matplotlib, seaborn, and Plotly guidance. The skill provides style presets, colorblind-safe palettes, figure sizing rules, and export helpers for manuscript figures.
skill.summary: Create journal-ready scientific figures with accessible styles and export settings.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Manual review found no evidence of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, network execution, or command execution. The static critical and high findings are false positives caused by hex color values, Markdown code fences, documentation URLs, and words such as key in normal plotting context. The remaining real risk is expected local file output from figure export and style template helpers.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Credential Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography and certificate or key material, but reviewed locations contain color palette hex values, style comments, or plotting parameter names. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, private keys, certificates, or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files contain visualization settings and documentation. Direct review found no secret file access or cryptographic API usage at the cited locations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code fences and inline examples for Python plotting workflows. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution, shell invocation, subprocess usage, or command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown formatting or literal Python examples. Repository search found no subprocess, os.system, popen, eval, or exec pattern.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Non-Executable References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to color palette and plotting documentation resources. The codebase contains no network client imports or runtime calls to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are citations and resource links. No requests, urllib, httpx, socket, or similar network code was found in reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Expected Local Figure and Style File Output
low_findings.description: The skill includes helper code that writes figures and matplotlib style templates to user-selected local paths. This is expected for a visualization export skill, but users should review output paths before running generated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file output is present and intentional. The behavior is local and user-directed, with no evidence of exfiltration or hidden writes.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Review
low_findings.description: The dangerous combination heuristic was triggered by accumulated false positives for URLs, Markdown code blocks, and hex colors. No evidence found of combined code execution, credential access, network transfer, obfuscation, or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review and targeted searches did not find the behaviors required for the heuristic. The cited context is audit output and normal documentation text.

content.value_statement: Researchers need figures that satisfy journal rules and remain accessible to readers. This skill provides reusable plotting guidance, style presets, color palettes, and export checks for manuscript-ready visuals.
actual_capabilities: Applies publication-focused matplotlib settings for fonts, line widths, spacing, and figure sizes.
Provides journal-oriented guidance for Nature, Science, Cell, PLOS, ACS, IEEE, and related publishers.
Uses Okabe-Ito, Wong, and Paul Tol palettes for colorblind-friendly categorical plots.
Guides exports to PDF, EPS, SVG, TIFF, and PNG with appropriate DPI settings.
Shows multi-panel figure patterns with panel labels, shared styling, and consistent layout.
Checks figure dimensions against common single-column and double-column requirements.
limitations: It does not validate the scientific correctness of source data or statistical tests.
It depends on local Python plotting libraries such as matplotlib, seaborn, Plotly, NumPy, and pandas.
Journal requirements can change, so final submissions still need publisher guideline review.
Complex image editing, microscopy processing, and graphical abstracts require separate tools.

use_cases.title: Prepare Manuscript Figures
use_cases.description: Create consistent figures that match target journal dimensions, formats, and accessibility expectations.

use_cases.title: Standardize Lab Visuals
use_cases.description: Use shared styles and color palettes across thesis figures, reports, posters, and presentations.

use_cases.title: Publish Technical Results
use_cases.description: Turn analysis outputs into clear plots for white papers, methods documents, and research reports.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Journal Plot
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one clean figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a publication-ready line plot for my time series data. Use a colorblind-safe palette, clear axis labels with units, and a single-column journal size.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Treatment Groups
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a box or violin plot comparing three treatment groups. Show individual points, use accessible colors, label the uncertainty, and export the figure for manuscript review.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Panel Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a four-panel figure with aligned panels, shared styling, panel labels, one heatmap, one scatter plot, and two summary plots. Follow Nature-style sizing.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Figure Submission Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my figure plan for journal submission. Check dimensions, resolution, file formats, font sizes, color accessibility, grayscale readability, legends, and panel labels.

output_examples.input: Create a Nature-style time course figure with two treatment groups and error bars.
output: A single-column figure with accessible blue and orange series.
Axis labels include units, and the legend uses a clean frame-free style.
The export plan includes a vector PDF and a high-resolution PNG.

output_examples.input: Prepare a four-panel figure for a manuscript results section.
output: A compact panel layout with labels A through D and consistent typography.
Each plot uses matched line widths, readable tick labels, and journal-aware spacing.
The checklist flags caption details, source data needs, and final size verification.

output_examples.input: Check whether my heatmap colors are suitable for publication.
output: The review recommends a perceptually uniform map for sequential data.
It warns against rainbow and red-green maps for accessibility reasons.
It suggests a labeled colorbar and grayscale review before submission.

best_practices: Confirm the target journal requirements before choosing dimensions, formats, and resolution.
Use accessible palettes and add markers, patterns, or labels when color alone is not enough.
Export plots in vector format for line art and high-resolution raster format for images.
anti_patterns: Using default plotting colors without checking colorblind accessibility.
Saving manuscript plots only as low-resolution screen images.
Adding decorative effects that distort values or reduce scientific clarity.

faq.question: Which plotting libraries does this skill support?
faq.answer: It focuses on matplotlib, seaborn, and Plotly workflows for scientific figures.

faq.question: Can it prepare figures for specific journals?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for common Nature, Science, Cell, PLOS, ACS, and IEEE requirements.

faq.question: Does it make figures colorblind-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends Okabe-Ito, Wong, Paul Tol, viridis, cividis, and other accessible options.

faq.question: Does it run statistical analysis?
faq.answer: No. It can show visual patterns, but users must choose and validate statistical methods separately.

faq.question: What file formats can it help export?
faq.answer: It covers PDF, EPS, SVG, TIFF, and PNG, with DPI guidance for each figure type.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish in the marketplace?
faq.answer: Manual review found no malicious behavior. The real risk is limited to expected local file output.
